display_name: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
role_summary: Terrell County, Georgia voter-registration organizers. Lucius Holloway Sr. (b. 1932): Korea-era Army veteran, post-office worker fired for movement activity, SCLC field organizer, and Terrell County NAACP first vice president since 1955. Emma Kate Holloway: Dawson beautician who sustained the family while the home was targeted in reprisal. Married 1957.
ai_reading: This joint interview is one of the few vantage points in the corpus on Terrell County, Georgia, the Albany-area county whose violence earned it the period nickname "Terrible Terrell," and it is recorded jointly with a husband and wife rather than the lone-organizer voice that carries most of the collection's field-secretary testimony. Where the better-known Georgia and Mississippi accounts narrate the courthouse and the mass meeting, the content of this entry is weighted toward the domestic interior: a gas tank cut on a cold night with an infant in the house, a hole sawn through a wall to fit a wood heater, a beautician's client list shrinking because white employers warned customers away from the Holloways. The collection's standard organizing narrative is told from the front of the church; Emma Kate Holloway's portion supplies the reprisal seen from the kitchen and the children's bench, the side of the campaign the field-secretary transcripts rarely hold. Read against the corpus, the page anchors the household cost of registration work as a theme distinct from the strategy-and-confrontation register that dominates the other Deep South entries. (Entry 141 is a newly ingested, single-pass transcript and has no precomputed semantic-neighbor or concept-axis placement yet, so this reading rests on the interview's own content and its place in the collection rather than on cosine-similarity neighbors.)
biographical_paragraph: Lucius Holloway Sr. was born in 1932 in Terrell County, Georgia, to sharecropping parents, Bobbie Holloway Sr. and Louise Thorne Holloway, and grew up farming cotton near Dawson before enlisting in the Army in June 1952 [src: 2]. Emma Kate Holloway was also born in Terrell County and was finishing high school in Dawson when the two met in 1955; they married in June 1957, and Lucius paid for her training as a beautician at Albany Technical College so she could open a shop while they raised four children [src: 2]. Their oral history, recorded jointly on March 9, 2013 in Albany, Georgia, is held by the Library of Congress as entry 141 of the Civil Rights History Project, the joint Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and Library of Congress initiative (AFC 2010/039) [src: 1, src: 2]. Lucius Holloway organized voter registration in Terrell County with the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and was fired from his post-office job for that work; he describes being named first vice president of the Terrell County NAACP in 1955, a position he says he still held at the time of the interview, and serving later on the Dawson City Council and the Terrell County Board of County Commissioners [src: 2]. Both Holloways recount sustained reprisals against their home, including a night their gas line was cut in winter with an infant in the house and an armed threat on Lucius's life the evening of a movement meeting, alongside Emma Kate's account of losing beautician clients after white employers warned the community away from the family [src: 2]. Lucius Holloway is the author of a memoir he cites in the interview as covering the Terrell County campaign [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Emma Kate Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Emma Kate Holloway explains why she built up a beautician's business while her husband organized: he had lost his federal job for the registration work.
interview_snippets.quote: he was fired from the post office because he was involved with voter stretching movement. He was, he was in the movement with SCLC and he was getting people's register to vote. So if we voted registered and voted, we would not have such a hard time because the white people didn't want you to register and vote because they weren't going to keep you down.

interview_snippets.speaker: Emma Kate Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Emma Kate Holloway describes the reprisals that followed the registration work, including a winter night when the family's gas line was cut.
interview_snippets.quote: He was threatened. They took the gas tank from our house that cold at night and we had it with our baby.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lucius Holloway, Sr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lucius Holloway dates his Terrell County NAACP leadership to the mid-1950s and says he held the office for the rest of his life.
interview_snippets.quote: The Krell's dinner in the NAACP appointed me his first vice president in 1955. A position that I have been in ever since.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lucius Holloway, Sr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lucius Holloway on continuing the registration work despite the threats against him.
interview_snippets.quote: So I stayed on course, spied all of the threats I had on my life.
